Metadata.Coram: General Division of the High Court — Originating Claim No 246 of 2023
Metadata.Tribunal_Court: General Division of the High Court of the Republic of Singapore

Party_Details.Name: Concorde Services Pte Ltd (in liquidation)

Facts.Text: On 26 May 2010, Mr Ong became a director and 50% shareholder of Concorde Services Pte Ltd.
In November 2011, Mr Ong and Mr Chua each deposited $100,000 into the Station 33 Bank Account as initial equity for Station 33.

From the start of 2012, cash payments from Station 33 customers were collected at the Premises by Mr Ong, Mr Peter Chong, or other hairstylists and were not deposited into the Station 33 Bank Account.
On 13 February 2012, Mr Ong signed a tenancy agreement with Ye Lizhen for J.C. Skin to sublet part of the Premises, with the agreement naming him personally as tenant.

Under the Subletting Agreement, rental cheques from J.C. Skin were issued to Mr Ong, with four months of rental totaling $20,000 transferred to the Station 33 Bank Account and the remaining rental amounts deposited into Mr Ong’s personal bank account.
On 25 April 2017, Andy Ong Beauty Services Pte Ltd was incorporated with Mr Ong’s involvement to operate a hair salon business.
On 19 October 2017, a lease between SMRT and Andy Ong Beauty Services Pte Ltd for the Premises commenced for 36 months, following the expiry of Concorde Services Pte Ltd's lease.

Party_Details.Issue: Whether the diversion of a company's cash takings, the retention of subletting rental income in a personal account, and the transfer of a corporate lease to a newly incorporated entity controlled by a director constitute actionable breaches of fiduciary duty and knowing receipt.
Party_Details.Rule: Directors owe strict fiduciary duties to their company to act in its best interests, to avoid conflicts of interest, and to preserve corporate assets and business opportunities. Diverting corporate cash takings, personally profiting from the subletting of corporate premises, and usurping lease renewals for the benefit of a separate, personally controlled entity are breaches of these duties, obligating the director to account for the diverted assets and profits.
Party_Details.Application: Concorde Services Pte Ltd operated a hair salon named Station 33 at a leased premises, funded by equal capital injections from two shareholders. One of the shareholders, Mr. Ong, managed the day-to-day operations. Following a breakdown in the relationship, Mr. Ong ceased depositing the salon's cash takings into the corporate bank account, disabled the CCTV cameras monitoring the cash register, and sublet a portion of the premises in his own name, depositing the bulk of the sublet rent into his personal account. Subsequently, prior to the expiration of Concorde's lease, Mr. Ong tendered for the lease renewal under Station 33's name but incorporated a new company, Andy Ong Beauty Services Pte Ltd, to ultimately assume the lease and take over the salon operations. Furthermore, corporate funds from the original Station 33 account were used to pay expenses for this new entity. The legal conflict assesses whether these orchestrated diversions of cash, lease rights, and business operations represent an unlawful misappropriation of Concorde's assets.
Party_Details.Judicial_Reasoning_Log: The court analyzed whether the first defendant owed fiduciary duties as a director of the claimant, rejected his claim of ignorance regarding his directorship, and held that he was aware of his role from the outset. The judge considered the disappearance of cash deposits after December 2011, the dismantling of CCTV cameras, the use of a personal tenancy agreement for subletting, and the routing of most subletting rental into the first defendant’s personal account as a coordinated pattern of conduct to siphon corporate wealth. The court found that the first defendant treated Station 33’s cash takings as his own, failed to account for them, and misapplied Station 33’s assets by transferring the business and goodwill to the second defendant. The court concluded that the first defendant breached fiduciary duties owed to the claimant, that the second defendant was a knowing recipient of misapplied assets, and that there was an unlawful means conspiracy. The limitation defense was dismissed because the first defendant operated as a Class 1 constructive trustee. The court assessed damages relating to cash takings, rental income from subletting, and the conversion of assets, entering judgment on liability and damages in favor of the claimant.

Metadata.Coram: General Division of the High Court — Originating Claim No 246 of 2023
Metadata.Tribunal_Court: General Division of the High Court of the Republic of Singapore

Party_Details.Name: Ong Kim Hock

Facts.Text: From 10 April 2002 to sometime in 2010, Mr Ong Kim Hock operated his own hairstyling studio under the name Andy Ong Hair Studio.
Mr Ong and Mr Chua became acquainted during Singapore national service and/or reservist obligations and later agreed around 2010 to start a hairstyling and salon business together.
Sometime in 2010, Mr Ong closed down Andy Ong Hair Studio and agreed with Mr Chua to start a new hairstyling and salon business.
On 26 May 2010, Mr Ong became a director and 50% shareholder of Concorde Services Pte Ltd.
By mid-June 2010, Mr Ong was a signatory to the Concorde Bank Account opened with United Overseas Bank Ltd for Concorde Services Pte Ltd.
From November 2011, Mr Ong was mainly responsible for Station 33’s day-to-day operations and worked as the head hairstylist at Station 33.
In November 2011, Mr Ong and Mr Chua each deposited $100,000 into the Station 33 Bank Account as initial equity for Station 33.

From the start of 2012, cash payments from Station 33 customers were collected at the Premises by Mr Ong, Mr Peter Chong, or other hairstylists and were not deposited into the Station 33 Bank Account.
On 13 February 2012, Mr Ong signed a tenancy agreement with Ye Lizhen for J.C. Skin to sublet part of the Premises, with the agreement naming him personally as tenant.

Under the Subletting Agreement, rental cheques from J.C. Skin were issued to Mr Ong, with four months of rental totaling $20,000 transferred to the Station 33 Bank Account and the remaining rental amounts deposited into Mr Ong’s personal bank account.
The latest available rental cheque from J.C. Skin to Mr Ong under the Subletting Agreement is dated 10 May 2013.

By early 2012, the original CCTV cameras at Station 33 near the sales counter and cash register were dismantled and replaced, and remote access previously available to Mr Chua was no longer available, while Mr Ong continued to manage the Premises.
After December 2011, Mr Ong introduced his acquaintance, Mr Peter Chong, to take over accounting work for Station 33, replacing Mdm Lim in that function.
In June 2012, a Station 33 payment voucher recorded that Mr Ong received $8,091.20 comprising $3,000 basic pay, $700 overtime, and $4,391.20 commission, before CPF deductions.
In December 2012, a Station 33 payment voucher recorded that Mr Ong received $6,160.75 comprising $3,000 basic pay and $3,160.75 commission, before CPF deductions.

On 20 April 2013, Mr Ong’s associate, Mr Peter Chong, emailed Station 33 financial documents including general ledgers and profit and loss statements to Mr Selvakumar while Mr Ong continued to manage Station 33’s operations.
In May 2014, Mr Ong arranged for the renewal of the Claimant’s Lease of the Premises for another 36 months from 16 October 2014 at a monthly rental of $17,000.

On 7 March 2015, Mr Chua sent a letter to Station 33 addressed to Mr Ong requesting Station 33’s financial and management records for 2012 to 2014, details of the 2014 lease renewal, details of the subletting arrangement, and employee records.
On 20 April 2015, Mr Chua sent a follow-up letter to Mr Ong granting 30 days to submit the requested Station 33 documents and indicating that further action would be considered for non-compliance.

On 15 July 2015, Mr Chua sent a letter to Mr Ong enclosing correspondence from the Inland Revenue Authority of Singapore and a State Courts summons relating to Concorde Services Pte Ltd’s tax and filing matters.

On 13 October 2017, Mr Chua sent a letter to Mr Ong stating that Station 33 records had not been submitted despite reminders and authorising the company’s accountant or auditor to take over all Station 33 documents and files on 14 October 2017.

On 3 April 2017, Mr Ong submitted a tender for the lease of the Premises under the name Station 33, indicating in the remarks that there would be a change of company name upon successful tender due to different ownership of the business.
On 25 April 2017, Andy Ong Beauty Services Pte Ltd was incorporated with Mr Ong’s involvement to operate a hair salon business.

After incorporation on 25 April 2017, the Accounting and Corporate Regulatory Authority profile of Andy Ong Beauty Services Pte Ltd was provided to SMRT to facilitate a lease agreement for the Premises.
Between 12 April and 22 May 2017, SMRT exchanged emails with Mr Ong and/or his nephew, Mr Johnson Ong, discussing key commercial terms for the tender of the Premises.

On 27 June 2017, SMRT sent a letter to Andy Ong Beauty Services Pte Ltd offering a lease of the Premises for 36 months from 19 October 2017 with monthly rental of $18,300 for the first year, $18,500 for the second year, and $18,700 for the third year, plus 0.5% of monthly gross sales.

On 19 October 2017, a lease between SMRT and Andy Ong Beauty Services Pte Ltd for the Premises commenced for 36 months, following a three-day fitting period for minor renovation and signboard changes.
From 19 October 2017, Mr Ong operated a hair salon business at the Premises through Andy Ong Beauty Services Pte Ltd, following the expiry of the Claimant’s Lease on 16 October 2017.

Party_Details.Issue: Whether a director breaches fiduciary duties by ceasing to deposit corporate cash takings, redirecting sublet rental income into personal accounts, paying himself unapproved remuneration, and ultimately usurping a corporate lease opportunity for a newly formed, personally controlled entity.
Party_Details.Rule: A director owes fiduciary duties to act honestly, protect corporate assets, and avoid conflicts of interest. These duties prohibit a director from treating the company's business income (including cash takings and subletting revenue) as his own, and from usurping corporate business opportunities—such as lease renewals for the company's premises—to benefit a separate, personally controlled business.
Party_Details.Application: Mr. Ong, holding a 50% share and managing the daily operations of Concorde's salon business, engaged in several unilateral actions regarding the company's finances and operations. He ceased depositing the salon's cash takings into the corporate bank account, disabled the CCTV system monitoring the cash register, and signed a subletting agreement for the corporate premises in his own name, routing the majority of the rental income into his personal account. He also disbursed remuneration to himself exceeding the agreed limits. As the corporate lease approached expiration, he tendered for the renewal initially under the company's name, but subsequently incorporated Andy Ong Beauty Services Pte Ltd to take over the lease and the salon's operations. The legal tension centers on whether these actions represent an actionable misappropriation of corporate funds, assets, and business opportunities, constituting a breach of fiduciary duty and an unlawful means conspiracy to strip Concorde of its business.
Party_Details.Judicial_Reasoning_Log: The court rejected Mr. Ong’s claim that he was unaware of his directorship in Concorde Services Pte Ltd, noting his own acknowledgments of the company’s role, his status as a bank signatory, and his statements about asking to be removed as a director. The judge found that the corporate structure did not disadvantage him and that his narrative of being misled was illogical. The court concluded that from early 2012 Mr. Ong deliberately took control of Station 33’s cash takings, stopped depositing cash into the corporate account, dismantled CCTV cameras to conceal the cash register, and arranged a subletting agreement in his personal name to siphon rental income into his personal account. The court also found that he paid himself unapproved, excessive remuneration. The judge preferred Mr. Chua’s evidence over Mr. Ong’s, rejecting the suggestion that Mr. Chua abandoned the business. The court held that Mr. Ong misapplied Station 33’s assets and unlawfully diverted the business and lease opportunities to his new entity, Andy Ong Beauty Services Pte Ltd. These actions constituted severe breaches of fiduciary duties and supported liability for knowing receipt and unlawful means conspiracy. The limitation defense was rejected as unpleaded and inapplicable because Mr. Ong acted as a Class 1 constructive trustee.

Metadata.Coram: General Division of the High Court — Originating Claim No 246 of 2023
Metadata.Tribunal_Court: General Division of the High Court of the Republic of Singapore

Party_Details.Name: Andy Ong Beauty Services Pte Ltd

Facts.Text: Andy Ong Beauty Services Pte Ltd was incorporated on 25 April 2017.
The Accounting and Corporate Regulatory Authority profile of Andy Ong Beauty Services Pte Ltd was provided to SMRT to facilitate the lease agreement for the Premises.
On 27 June 2017, SMRT sent a letter addressed to the second defendant regarding an offer for the lease of the Premises.
A lease agreement was entered into between SMRT and the second defendant for 36 months from 19 October 2017.
The second defendant’s lease of the Premises commenced on 19 October 2017.
On 4 October 2020, the Second Defendant’s Lease was renewed for another 36 months from 19 October 2020 to 18 October 2023, at a discounted rental rate due to the COVID-19 pandemic.
On 11 May 2023, the Second Defendant’s Lease was renewed again for another 36 months from 19 October 2023 to 18 October 2026 for $13,688 per month.

Party_Details.Issue: Whether a newly incorporated entity that secures a lease originally held by a related company and receives operational funding originating from that company's accounts is liable for knowing receipt or unlawful means conspiracy.
Party_Details.Rule: A corporate entity that receives assets, business opportunities, or payments traceable to another company's business, with knowledge of the controlling director's breach of fiduciary duty in diverting those assets, may be held liable for knowing receipt and participation in an unlawful means conspiracy to strip the original company of its business.
Party_Details.Application: A hair salon's lease expired, and a new lease for the same premises was immediately granted to a newly incorporated entity. The dispute involves whether the use of the original company's bank account to pay the new entity's operational expenses constitutes an actionable diversion of corporate value.
Party_Details.Judicial_Reasoning_Log: The court treated Andy Ong Beauty Services Pte Ltd as the corporate vehicle through which Mr. Ong unlawfully continued the hair salon business after letting the claimant's lease expire. The judge found that the tender for the premises was submitted under the old company's name (Station 33) with a deliberate indication of a forthcoming name change to facilitate the transition. The second defendant was incorporated shortly thereafter and granted the lease, seamlessly usurping the business opportunity. The court also observed that the second defendant's operational expenses were being paid from the original Station 33 Bank Account. Concluding that the second defendant received business, assets, and financial benefits traceable directly to the claimant through the director's breaches of duty, the judge held the entity liable as a knowing recipient and as a co-conspirator in an unlawful means conspiracy with Mr. Ong.
